metadata.description: Terminal storage economics for cement and clinker at US port and inland terminals. Covers major storage types with capital costs, operating costs, and throughput capacity.
sources: Portland Cement Association (PCA) Plant Design Guidelines
ASCE Civil Engineering construction cost indices

storage_types.description: Welded steel cylindrical silo with cone bottom, 5,000 ton nominal capacity. Common at truck distribution terminals and small rail-served facilities.
storage_types.throughput_notes: Assumes ~1 complete fill/empty cycle per month
advantages: Lower capital cost per unit
Faster construction timeline (3-6 months)
Modular - can add additional silos as demand grows
Proven technology with simple maintenance
Small footprint suitable for constrained terminal sites
disadvantages: Limited capacity per unit
Higher cost per ton at small scale
Steel corrosion risk in humid/coastal environments
Interior coating required for cement compatibility
Flow problems possible with cement bridging in cone bottom

storage_types.description: Large welded steel cylindrical silo with cone or flat bottom, 10,000-25,000 ton capacity. Used at major import terminals and large distribution hubs.
storage_types.throughput_notes: Higher throughput due to vessel-scale receiving and continuous truck/barge loading
advantages: Better economies of scale than small silos
Can receive full vessel parcels without overflow
Higher fill and discharge rates support vessel discharge schedules
Multiple discharge points for simultaneous truck and barge loading
Flat-bottom option with reclaim system maximizes usable volume
disadvantages: Higher absolute capital cost
Longer construction timeline (6-12 months)
Foundation requirements more substantial
Flat-bottom requires mechanical reclaim (sweep auger or airslide)
Single-point failure risk if only one large silo vs. multiple small

storage_types.description: Concrete dome storage structure, 50,000-100,000 ton capacity. Used at major production plants and large-scale import terminals for bulk clinker or cement storage.
storage_types.throughput_notes: High capacity supports large vessel parcels and continuous outbound distribution. Typical 8-10 fills per year.
advantages: Lowest cost per ton of capacity at scale
Extremely durable (50+ year lifespan)
Weather-resistant and climate-controlled interior
Low visual profile compared to equivalent silo farm
Can store clinker, cement, or other bulk powders
Minimal maintenance requirements
disadvantages: Very high absolute capital cost
Long construction timeline (12-18 months)
Requires mechanical reclaim system (portal or bridge reclaimer)
Not easily expanded once built
Large footprint requirement
Specialized contractor required for dome construction

storage_types.description: Enclosed flat-floor warehouse with front-end loader or overhead crane reclaim. Used for temporary storage, overflow, or multi-product terminals.
storage_types.throughput_notes: Limited by loader reclaim rate. Good for slower-moving products or overflow.
advantages: Flexible multi-product storage (partition for different cement types)
Can be converted from existing warehouse buildings
Simple reclaim with standard front-end loaders
Easy to inspect and sample stored product
Lower foundation requirements than silos
disadvantages: Lower utilization of floor space (limited pile height)
Higher labor cost due to loader operations
Product segregation risk with multiple products
Moisture intrusion risk at floor level
Dust management more challenging than enclosed silos
Not suitable for finished cement long-term (moisture/hardening)

storage_types.description: Open-air paved storage pad for clinker only. Clinker is weather-resistant and can be stored outdoors. Not suitable for finished cement.
storage_types.throughput_notes: High throughput limited only by stacking/reclaim equipment capacity.
advantages: Lowest cost storage option by far
Virtually unlimited scalability (add more pad area)
Simple construction (grading + paving)
Easy access for inspection and sampling
Clinker is weatherproof - no degradation from rain/sun
Can use standard construction equipment for stacking/reclaim
disadvantages: Clinker only - NOT suitable for finished cement or fly ash
Large land area requirement
Stormwater runoff management required (alkaline runoff)
Dust from wind erosion requires suppression
Contamination risk from adjacent operations
Seasonal ground freezing can create reclaim difficulties

storage_planning_notes.buffer_stock_guidance: Import terminals should maintain 15-30 days of average throughput as buffer stock to accommodate vessel scheduling variability.
storage_planning_notes.vessel_parcel_sizing: Storage capacity should accommodate at least one full vessel parcel (25,000-50,000 tons for Supramax) plus working inventory.
storage_planning_notes.product_segregation: Terminals handling multiple cement types (Type I, Type II, Type I/II, Type V, slag cement) require separate silos for each product to avoid contamination.
storage_planning_notes.seasonal_considerations: Northern terminals should pre-build inventory in Q4 for spring construction season demand surge (March-June).
